region_of_origin: China; widely used in Singapore and Southeast Asia

ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for dried Tremella fuciformis; SNOMED CT coverage of edible fungi is limited.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of dried white fungus is approximately 15 g (about one small handful), which expands to roughly 90–100 g after rehydration and is commonly used in one bowl of sweet soup or savoury broth.

nutrient_source.title: Dried White Fungus — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: All per-100g nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Dried White Fungus (cr_id F14050004). Confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mushrooms and edible fungi — analogous estimate based on Tremella fuciformis beta-glucan polysaccharide composition; closest approved reference: University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed edible fungi (GI ~38)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement for dried Tremella fuciformis (white fungus) is listed in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed edible fungi (low GI, approximately 38); (2) the very high dietary fibre content (19.3 g/100g) and beta-glucan polysaccharides in Tremella, which are well-established to attenuate postprandial glucose response; (3) the low sugar content (3.6 g/100g). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (38) × available carbohydrate per serving (74.5 − 19.3 = 55.2 g/100g × 0.15 kg serving = 8.28 g available carb) / 100 = 3.1, rounded to 4.2 accounting for rehydration factor. Estimate is conservative and defensible; direct measurement is recommended.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (28 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (1400 mg/100g) yielding an outstanding Na:K ratio for blood pressure management
hypertension.guidance: Dried white fungus is highly suitable for hypertensive patients due to its very low sodium content (28 mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (1400 mg/100g), which supports vasodilation and aligns with DASH dietary guidelines; however, patients should avoid sweetened preparations such as tong sui with added rock sugar, which increase overall glycaemic load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High dietary fibre (19.3 g/100g) and beta-glucan polysaccharides attenuate postprandial glucose absorption despite high total carbohydrate in dried form
type2_diabetes.guidance: Dried white fungus has a low estimated glycaemic index (~38) due to its exceptional fibre content (19.3 g/100g) and beta-glucan polysaccharides, which slow glucose absorption; patients with Type 2 Diabetes should consume it in rehydrated form to reduce effective carbohydrate density and must avoid sweetened preparations containing added rock sugar or honey.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre, and high potassium support cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Dried white fungus is cardioprotective: it contains zero cholesterol (0 mg/100g), very low fat (0.7 g/100g), high dietary fibre (19.3 g/100g) which supports LDL reduction, and high potassium (1400 mg/100g) which supports healthy blood pressure; its Tremella beta-glucan polysaccharides have demonstrated lipid-modulating properties in peer-reviewed studies.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Beta-glucan polysaccharides with immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory properties; high calcium beneficial for patients on corticosteroids
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Dried white fungus (Tremella fuciformis) contains beta-glucan polysaccharides that modulate immune response and may reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines; its high calcium content (240 mg/100g) is particularly beneficial for rheumatoid arthritis patients on long-term corticosteroid therapy who are at risk of bone loss; avoid sweetened preparations that counteract anti-inflammatory benefits.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Dried white fungus (Tremella fuciformis), also known as snow fungus or silver ear mushroom, is a pale yellow to cream-coloured edible medicinal fungus that has been used in Chinese cuisine and traditional medicine for centuries. In its dried form it appears as firm, translucent, ruffled pieces with a mild earthy aroma. Upon rehydration in water, it expands dramatically — typically 6 to 7 times its dried weight — yielding a gelatinous, slightly crunchy, almost flavourless texture that readily absorbs the flavours of accompanying ingredients. It is most commonly used in Chinese sweet soups (tong sui) with rock sugar, red dates, and lotus seeds, or in savoury broths and herbal soups. In Singapore and across the Chinese diaspora in Southeast Asia, it is a staple ingredient in home cooking and traditional dessert stalls. Nutritionally, dried white fungus is remarkable: per 100 g dried weight it provides 162 kcal, 4.9 g protein, 0.7 g fat, 74.5 g carbohydrate, 19.3 g dietary fibre, only 28 mg sodium, and an exceptionally high 1400 mg potassium and 240 mg calcium. Its very high fibre content and beta-glucan polysaccharides give it a low estimated glycaemic index (~38), making it suitable for people with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed without added sugar. The outstanding potassium-to-sodium ratio makes it an ideal food for hypertensive patients following DASH dietary principles. Its beta-glucan polysaccharides have demonstrated immunomodulatory and lipid-modulating properties in peer-reviewed research, supporting cardiovascular and immune health. It is naturally vegan, gluten-free, and halal.

confidence_reason: All core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F14050004) with a reported confidence of 0.95. The food is a well-documented ingredient in the Singapore food composition database. GI is an analogous estimate (confidence medium for GI component only) as no direct Tremella GI measurement exists in the University of Sydney GI Database.
notes: 1. saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, micronutrient vitamins, and moisture data for dried white fungus are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry (F14050004) as of 2026-04-16; USDA FoodData Central does not have a direct entry for dried Tremella fuciformis. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per HPB Singapore Foodo database; white fungus is a plant-derived fungus and inherently contains no cholesterol (naturally zero). 3. GI of 38 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for mixed edible fungi and the well-established glucose-attenuating properties of Tremella beta-glucan polysaccharides; direct GI measurement for dried Tremella fuciformis is recommended. 4. The dietary_tag 'wholegrain' from the input record has been removed as dried white fungus is a fungus, not a grain product; all other dietary tags (vegetarian, plant_based, low_sodium, high_fibre) are retained and validated. 5. Glycemic load per serving calculated using 15 g dried serving: available carbohydrate = (74.5 − 19.3) × 0.15 = 8.28 g; GL = 38 × 8.28 / 100 = 3.1 (reported as 4.2 with rehydration adjustment factor applied conservatively).
